skill.name: azure-resource-manager-durabletask-dotnet
skill.description: Guides .NET developers through Azure Resource Manager operations for Durable Task Scheduler resources, including schedulers, task hubs, retention policies, authentication, and SDK usage.
skill.summary: Manage Azure Durable Task Scheduler resources with the .NET Azure Resource Manager SDK.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, credential, weak-crypto, and combination findings, but review shows the command and crypto hits are Markdown documentation false positives. The remaining risk is medium because the skill teaches Azure management-plane operations, uses Azure credential environment variables, and includes examples that can create, update, and delete cloud resources if copied and run.

medium_findings.title: Azure Management Operations Can Modify Cloud Resources
medium_findings.description: The skill provides examples for creating, updating, and deleting Durable Task Scheduler resources through Azure Resource Manager. This is legitimate DevOps guidance, but copied examples can affect live cloud infrastructure and costs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly call Azure Resource Manager create, update, and delete APIs. There is no malicious intent, but the operational impact is real if run against a production subscription.

medium_findings.title: Credential Environment Variables Documented
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Azure service principal variables, including a client secret placeholder, and reads subscription and resource group values from the environment. This is common Azure SDK practice, but users must avoid exposing secrets in shared sessions or logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains explicit Azure credential variable names and environment reads. I found no evidence that the skill exfiltrates or prints secret values.

low_findings.title: Command Detections Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The external command findings are documentation examples for installing .NET packages or inline API names. They are not executable skill scripts and do not show command injection or hidden execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The detected commands appear inside Markdown code blocks or tables. The skill repository contains only SKILL.md, so there is no supporting executable wrapper.

low_findings.title: Network Indicators Are Benign Documentation Values
low_findings.description: The hardcoded IP values are private CIDR examples for an Azure allowlist, and the hardcoded URLs point to official GitHub and NuGet references. No outbound request code or data transfer behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The IPs are RFC1918-style example network ranges used in allowlist configuration. The URLs are source references, not destinations for runtime exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And Combination Heuristics Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm hits align with documentation terms such as SDK and SKU, not cryptographic API usage. The dangerous combination heuristic combines unrelated documentation examples and is not supported by malicious behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no hashing, encryption, obfuscation, download, or execution logic. The file is instructional Markdown with Azure SDK examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Analyzer Command Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The analyzer reported Ruby or shell-style command execution at many Markdown backtick locations. Review confirms these are fenced documentation blocks and inline identifiers, not code executed by the skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The repeated locations correspond to Markdown code fences and tables. No executable file or runtime hook exists in the scanned file set.

dangerous_patterns.title: Azure Credential Variable Access Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The analyzer correctly identified Azure credential variable names. The usage is for standard authentication setup and contains placeholders rather than real secrets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact credential variable names are present in the file. The risk is limited because there is no code that transmits or logs the secret value.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Reference Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The analyzer correctly found network-looking values, but they are private IP allowlist examples and official documentation links. No suspicious remote endpoint or webhook pattern was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network values are explained by surrounding Azure allowlist and source reference text. I found no evidence of data exfiltration behavior.

content.value_statement: Provisioning Durable Task Scheduler resources through Azure Resource Manager can be hard to map from SDK types to real workflows. This skill gives .NET examples for schedulers, task hubs, retention policies, authentication, and cleanup.
actual_capabilities: Explains the management-plane role of Azure.ResourceManager.DurableTask.
Shows package installation and Azure Identity authentication setup.
Provides examples for creating Dedicated and Consumption schedulers.
Shows task hub creation, scheduler listing, updates, and deletion.
Summarizes key SDK resource types and extension methods.
limitations: Does not start orchestrations or manage Durable Task runtime instances.
Does not verify live Azure subscription permissions or quotas.
Uses illustrative resource names and locations that users must adapt.
Does not replace Azure security review for production deployments.

use_cases.title: Provision a Scheduler
use_cases.description: Create a Durable Task Scheduler and task hub with the .NET Azure Resource Manager SDK.

use_cases.title: Review ARM SDK Types
use_cases.description: Map Durable Task Scheduler resources to the correct SDK classes, collections, and extension methods.

use_cases.title: Prepare Deployment Automation
use_cases.description: Draft idempotent management-plane code for scheduler setup, updates, retention policy work, and cleanup.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Scheduler
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the Azure Resource Manager DurableTask .NET skill, outline the steps to create a Consumption scheduler and one task hub.

prompt_templates.title: Add Authentication Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me configure DefaultAzureCredential for Durable Task Scheduler management in .NET using environment variables safely.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Scheduler SKUs
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare Dedicated and Consumption Durable Task Scheduler SKUs for a .NET service with variable workload volume.

prompt_templates.title: Design Deployment Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a deployment plan for creating, updating, validating, and cleaning up Durable Task Scheduler resources with Azure.ResourceManager.DurableTask.

output_examples.input: I need to create a production Durable Task Scheduler.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step plan covering package setup, DefaultAzureCredential, resource group lookup, Dedicated SKU selection, IP allowlist review, task hub creation, and error handling.

output_examples.input: I am confused about management plane versus data plane.
output_examples.output: A concise explanation that management-plane SDKs provision schedulers and hubs, while data-plane SDKs start and query orchestrations.
A cleanup sequence that deletes task hubs before schedulers and warns to confirm subscription, resource group, and environment before running deletion operations.

best_practices: Confirm the Azure subscription and resource group before running create, update, or delete examples.
Use DefaultAzureCredential and keep service principal secrets out of prompts and logs.
Treat IP allowlists, retention policies, and SKU capacity as production design decisions.
anti_patterns: Do not use this management-plane skill to start or query orchestrations.
Do not copy deletion examples into production automation without safeguards.
Do not hardcode Azure client secrets or subscription-specific values in source code.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps with Azure Resource Manager operations for Durable Task Scheduler resources in .NET.

faq.question: Is this for starting orchestrations?
faq.answer: No. It covers management-plane resources, not Durable Task data-plane orchestration calls.

faq.question: Which authentication approach does it show?
faq.answer: It shows DefaultAzureCredential with Azure environment variables and Azure.Identity.

faq.question: Can it create task hubs?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes task hub creation under a Durable Task Scheduler resource.

faq.question: Does it include cleanup guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows deleting task hubs before deleting the parent scheduler.

faq.question: What should users verify before running examples?
faq.answer: Users should verify subscription, resource group, permissions, SKU choice, region, and deletion impact.
